content:
# Leviticus Chapter 22 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 71r–71v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_022_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_022_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** The LORD spoke to Moses: **2** "Tell Aaron and his sons to handle the sacred offerings of the Israelites with care — the offerings they consecrate to me — so that they do not profane my holy name. I am the LORD.

**3** "Tell them: For all your future generations, if any of your descendants approaches the sacred offerings that the Israelites consecrate to the LORD while he is unclean, that person must be cut off from my presence. I am the LORD.

**4** "No descendant of Aaron who has a skin disease or a bodily discharge may eat the sacred offerings until he is clean. Anyone who touches something made unclean by a corpse, or who has had a seminal emission, **5** or who touches any swarming creature that makes him unclean, or touches a person whose uncleanness — whatever it may be — makes him unclean — **6** any such person who touches these things will be unclean until evening. He must not eat the sacred offerings until he has bathed himself in water. **7** When the sun has set and he is clean again, he may then eat the sacred offerings, for they are his food. **8** He must not eat anything found dead or torn by wild animals — it would make him unclean through it. I am the LORD.

**9** "The priests must keep my charge, or they will bear the sin and die for it when they profane it. I am the LORD who makes them holy.

**10** "No outsider may eat the sacred offering. No guest staying with a priest and no hired worker may eat it. **11** But if a priest buys a slave with his own money, or if a slave is born in his household, that person may eat his food. **12** If a priest's daughter marries an outsider, she may not eat from the sacred contributions. **13** But if a priest's daughter becomes widowed or divorced, with no children, and returns to live in her father's house as in her youth — she may eat her father's food. No outsider may eat it.

**14** "If anyone eats a sacred offering by mistake, he must make it right with the priest — replacing it with something equivalent and adding a fifth of its value on top. **15** The priests must not allow the sacred offerings of the Israelites to be treated as common things; **16** they must not bring guilt upon themselves by letting people eat the sacred offerings — for I am the LORD who makes them holy."

**17** The LORD spoke to Moses: **18** "Speak to Aaron and his sons and to all the Israelites and say to them: When anyone from the house of Israel or any foreigner living in Israel brings a burnt offering — whether to fulfill a vow or as a freewill offering — presenting it to the LORD, **19** you must present a male without defect from the cattle, the sheep, or the goats, if you want it accepted. **20** Do not bring anything with a defect, because it will not be accepted on your behalf.

**21** "When anyone presents a fellowship offering to the LORD to fulfill a special vow or as a freewill offering — whether from the herd or the flock — it must be without defect to be accepted. It must have no flaw at all. **22** Do not offer to the LORD anything that is blind, injured, or maimed, anything with running sores, a rash, or festering skin. Do not place any of these on the altar as a food offering to the LORD. **23** You may, however, use a bull or lamb that has a limb too long or too short as a freewill offering; but for a vow it will not be accepted. **24** You must not offer to the LORD an animal with damaged, crushed, torn, or cut genitals. You must not do this anywhere in your land. **25** And you must not accept such animals from a foreigner to offer as the food of your God. Since they are corrupt — they have a defect — they will not be accepted on your behalf."

**26** The LORD spoke to Moses: **27** "When a calf, a lamb, or a goat is born, it must remain with its mother for seven days. From the eighth day on, it will be acceptable as a food offering to the LORD. **28** But do not slaughter a cow or a sheep along with its young on the same day.

**29** "When you sacrifice a thanksgiving offering to the LORD, offer it in a way that it will be accepted on your behalf. **30** It must be eaten that same day — leave none of it until morning. I am the LORD.

**31** "Keep my commands and carry them out. I am the LORD. **32** Do not profane my holy name, for I must be treated as holy among the Israelites. I am the LORD who makes you holy — **33** who brought you out of Egypt to be your God. I am the LORD."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### What Leviticus 22 Is Actually About

Leviticus 22 is a chapter about access — who gets to touch the holy, when, and under what conditions. It draws three concentric circles: the priests and their qualification to eat sacred food (vv.1–16), the animals presented at the altar and their qualification for offering (vv.17–25), and a pair of compassion-laws about timing (vv.26–30). Then it ends with one of the most theologically charged climaxes in the entire Holiness Code (vv.31–33).

The chapter follows directly from chapter 21, which asked: which priests may serve at the altar? Chapter 22 asks: which food may the qualified priest eat, and which animals may anyone bring to the altar? The progression is deliberate. Holiness flows from the center outward — from the divine presence, to the altar, to the priests, to the sacrificial animals, to the people who bring them. Every link in the chain must be intact.

### Verses 1–9: Priests, Impurity, and Sacred Food

The opening section guards one of the most intimate acts in the sacrificial system: the priest eating the holy food. In Leviticus's theological architecture, this eating is not merely a practical matter of priestly compensation. It is a sharing in the sacrifice — a participation in the meal that the offering has become. When the priest eats the holy portion, he is, in some sense, ingesting the sacred. The conditions that must surround that act are therefore stringent.

Verse 2 uses the phrase *yinnazru* — from the root *nzr*, the same root behind *Nazirite* and the *nezer* (crown) of the high priest in chapter 21. The word is translated here as "handle with care," but the root meaning is "to separate oneself from, to consecrate." The priests are told not merely to be careful around sacred things but to maintain the posture of consecrated separation from them — which is, paradoxically, the posture that enables full participation in them. You cannot rightly draw near to what is holy unless you are able to approach from the position of consecration.

The list of disqualifying conditions in verses 4–6 maps onto the impurity laws of chapters 11–15: skin disease (*tzara'at*, the same condition discussed at length in chapters 13–14), bodily discharge (chapters 15), contact with a corpse (Numbers 19), seminal emission (chapter 15), contact with swarming creatures (chapter 11), and contact with a person in an unclean state (chapter 15). The priest who bears any of these conditions is not morally guilty — he has simply passed through a state that places him outside the category of clean, and the holy food requires that he be clean before he eats.

The time-boundary matters: he is unclean "until evening." Sunset is the hinge of the Hebrew day. It closes one day and opens the next. The impurity that follows contact with any of these conditions is not permanent — it ends with the day. The priest bathes, the sun sets, and he is clean again. The system is demanding but not crushing: the path back to qualification is clearly marked.

Verse 9 introduces a note of gravity that lifts the section from mere regulation: "The priests must keep my charge, or they will bear the sin and die for it when they profane it." The word for "profane" here is *chalal* — to pierce, to make hollow, to treat as common what is sacred. The warning is judicial, but the language is theological. To handle holy things carelessly is not merely a procedural violation; it is an act of conceptual destruction, a piercing of the sacred membrane that separates the holy from the common. The stakes of priestly carelessness are not administrative — they are theological and, apparently, mortal.

### Verses 10–16: The Priest's Table and Its Restricted Guest List

Who may eat at the priestly table? The category of "outsider" (*zar*) — the non-priest — is the baseline exclusion. The sacred food belongs to those set apart for priestly service; it is not available to guests or hired workers who happen to be staying in the priestly household. This is not inhospitality; it is the maintenance of a category. The food is not ordinary food. To treat it as such — by sharing it with anyone who happens to be around — is to perform the very profanation the chapter is trying to prevent.

The exception for slaves purchased or born in the household (v.11) reflects the ancient understanding that the household slave is an extension of the household in a way that a hired worker is not. The hired worker is temporarily present; he will leave. The purchased slave or native-born slave belongs to the household in a more constitutive sense. The slave's status derives from the master's status — and since the master is a priest, the slave shares in the priestly household's access to holy food.

The rule about the priest's daughter (vv.12–13) is among the most pastorally nuanced regulations in the chapter. A priest's daughter who marries a non-priest loses her priestly food access — she belongs now to a different household. But if she is widowed or divorced and returns to her father's house, with no children to anchor her in her married family, she may eat her father's food again. The text recognizes her vulnerability — she has returned to the only household that can sustain her — and it restores her priestly access along with her domestic belonging.

The exception for eating sacred food "by mistake" (*bishgagah*, v.14) reveals that the regulations are not designed to trap the unwary. If someone eats the sacred food unknowingly, the remedy is restoration plus a twenty-percent surcharge. The system has built-in repair mechanisms. The goal is not punishment for ignorance; it is the correction of what has been disrupted. The holy can be restored to its proper status through the right response to violation.

### Verses 17–25: The Unblemished Sacrifice

The second major section shifts from those who eat the offering to the offering itself. The primary rule is stated with elegant simplicity: **the sacrificial animal must be without defect** (*tamim* — complete, whole, unimpaired). This requirement applies to burnt offerings, freewill offerings, and fellowship offerings brought by both Israelites and foreigners living among them.

The blemish list of verses 21–24 corresponds to the priest blemish list of chapter 21:17–23. Blind, injured, maimed, running sores, rash, festering skin, damaged genitals — none of these may appear on the altar. The parallel is deliberate and deeply meaningful. Both the priest who officiates and the animal that is offered must be *tamim*: without blemish, complete. The reason is not aesthetic — it is symbolic-theological. Both are representing something before God. The priest represents the people; the animal represents the person offering it. And what is offered to the LORD must embody the quality of wholeness that the encounter with holiness demands.

The exception at verse 23 is careful and specific: an animal with a limb too long or too short may be offered as a freewill offering but not for a vow. A freewill offering is an act of spontaneous generosity — the worshiper gives what he has, out of desire rather than obligation. A vow is a specific promise: the worshiper committed before God to bring something of a particular quality, and he is bound to that commitment. The exception acknowledges the difference between spontaneous giving (which receives more latitude) and deliberate promise (which must be kept exactly as made).

Verse 24's specific prohibition against offering animals with damaged, crushed, torn, or cut genitals — and the instruction that this must not be practiced "anywhere in your land" — addresses a known practice in ancient Near Eastern religion. Castrated or otherwise genitally damaged animals were sometimes offered in surrounding cults. The law marks a boundary: the offering brought to Israel's God must not bear the marks of pagan ritual practice. The animal's reproductive wholeness is part of its *tamim* — its completeness before the LORD.

The extension of these rules to foreigners (v.25) is significant. Leviticus consistently applies its standards universally within the community of Israel — both native Israelites and resident foreigners are bound by the same sacrificial requirements. The altar is not a space where foreigners receive a reduced standard. If a foreigner brings an offering, it must meet the same qualifications as one brought by a native Israelite. The LORD's standards are not ethnically relative.

### Verses 26–30: The Compassion Laws

After the blemish regulations comes a pair of laws about timing that carry a distinctly different emotional register: **verse 27** (the newborn animal must remain with its mother for seven days before being eligible for sacrifice) and **verse 28** (a cow or sheep and its young must not be slaughtered on the same day).

The seven-day rule for newborn animals mirrors several other seven-day waiting periods in the Pentateuch. The human male child is circumcised on the eighth day (Lev 12:3); the newborn animal becomes eligible for sacrifice on the eighth day. The parallel suggests a shared logic: the first seven days of life are a period of completion, of coming fully into existence. Only after this initial week of life is the creature fully constituted as a member of its kind — and only then can it legitimately be offered.

The mother-offspring law of verse 28 — *oto ve-et-beno lo tishchatu beyom echad*, "it and its young you shall not slaughter on the same day" — is one of the most compassion-focused laws in the Pentateuch. Maimonides argued that this law, like the prohibition on boiling a kid in its mother's milk (Exod 23:19; 34:26; Deut 14:21), protects the feelings of the mother animal. The pain of losing one's young is, in some sense, felt even by animals; the law refuses to make a creature witness its offspring's death. Nahmanides went further: the prohibition exists to prevent the hardening of the human heart. When people become capable of killing a mother and child in the same moment, without pause, a kind of cruelty is cultivated that will eventually extend beyond animals.

Whether or not one accepts either reading, the law creates a pause in the middle of the sacrificial system. Even in the context of animal slaughter — which is permitted, regulated, and even required — there is a limit imposed by something like compassion. The system that takes animal life does not become indifferent to it. The creature that gives its young to the altar is not required to watch.

The thanksgiving offering law of verse 30 — eat it the same day, leave none until morning — connects to the earlier regulations of chapter 7 (the thanksgiving offering must be eaten on the day of sacrifice). The logic is similar to the Passover and the manna: the holy moment is now; the provision must be received in its time; nothing is to be hoarded or deferred. The thanksgiving offering, specifically, has this quality — it arises from a specific moment of deliverance or joy, and it is consumed in that moment, fully, rather than spread across multiple days.

### Verses 31–33: The Theological Climax

The chapter ends with a four-part declaration that is simultaneously a command, a self-identification, an act of grace, and a covenant anchor:

**"Keep my commands and carry them out."** The same framing that appears throughout the Holiness Code (19:37; 20:22; 26:3). The laws are not optional; they are the structure within which Israel's relationship with the holy God operates.

**"Do not profane my holy name."** The prohibition against profaning the divine name — *lo techalelu et-shem qodshi* — is one of the most radical statements in the chapter. The name of God can be profaned. The holiness of God is, in some sense, vulnerable to what Israel does. This is a stunning theological claim: when Israel violates the laws of the Holiness Code, something happens to God's name in the world. The name becomes *chalal* — pierced, hollow, treated as common. This is the stakes of Israel's obedience, not merely individual blessing or punishment, but the reputation of the holy name among the nations.

**"For I must be treated as holy among the Israelites."** The verb here is *ve-niqdashthi* — from the same root *q-d-sh* (holy). Literally: "I will be made holy" or "I am to be hallowed." God's holiness is something that is enacted, performed, demonstrated in the world through Israel's life. This is the inverse of profanation. When Israel lives according to the Holiness Code, God's name is hallowed among them. When Israel treats the holy as common, God's name is profaned. The community's holiness is the vehicle by which divine holiness becomes visible.

**"I am the LORD who makes you holy — who brought you out of Egypt to be your God. I am the LORD."** The final verse is the gravitational center of the entire chapter and one of the most important verses in Leviticus. Three declarations are compressed into a single breath:

1. *I am the LORD who makes you holy.* The source of Israel's holiness is not Israel's effort but divine action. *Mekadishkhem* — the Sanctifier — is a divine title. God is not responding to Israel's achieved holiness by recognizing it; God is creating Israel's holiness by an active, ongoing work. The present participle communicates continuing activity.

2. *Who brought you out of Egypt.* The holiness laws are not arbitrary divine preferences. They are grounded in historical action — in the Exodus, the defining event of Israel's identity. The God who demands holiness is the God who broke the chains. The ethical claims of Leviticus rest on the foundation of liberation. You do not keep these laws to earn your freedom; you keep them because you are already free.

3. *To be your God.* The purpose of the Exodus was not merely rescue — it was relationship. The liberation from Egypt was *lihyot lakhem l'Elohim* — "to be your God." God brought Israel out in order to be God-to-them, in order to enter into the covenantal intimacy that the entire sacrificial system exists to sustain and enact. The laws of purity and sacrifice are not obstacles to relationship; they are the structure of the relationship.

The triple formula *ani YHWH* — "I am the LORD" — appears in verses 2, 3, 8, 9, 16, 30, 31, 32, and 33. In chapter 22 alone it tolls nine times. This is not filler. This is the chapter's heartbeat. Every regulation, every restriction, every exception is bracketed and grounded by the same self-declaration: *I am the LORD.* The laws do not exist in an abstract ethical space; they exist in the context of a divine identity that is being disclosed. The regulations of chapter 22 are not merely hygienic or administrative. They are the shape that relationship with *this particular God* takes in practice.

### The LXX in Vaticanus (Pages 108–109)

Codex Vaticanus pages 108–109 preserve the Greek of Leviticus 22 in generally good condition. The pages visible in Vaticanus correspond to both folios of the chapter, with chapter 22 opening mid-page 108 (following the end of chapter 21) and concluding on page 109.

Several LXX translation choices illuminate the chapter's theology:

The phrase *yinnazru* in verse 2 is rendered in the LXX as *proseche thosin* — "they shall take heed" or "they shall be careful." The Greek translators did not reach for the *nazir*-language of consecration that the Hebrew *nzr* root carries; they chose a practical watchfulness term instead. This is a notable flattening — the Hebrew suggests a posture of set-apart distance that enables proper approach; the Greek renders it simply as careful attention. The theology of the passage is preserved, but one of its more layered dimensions is smoothed.

The term *tamim* (without blemish, complete, whole) is rendered consistently as *amomon* or *holokleron* — "without blemish" and "whole/entire" respectively. *Amomon* is the root of the New Testament *amomos* (blameless, without blemish) used in Ephesians 1:4 ("chosen in him before the foundation of the world to be holy and *blameless*"), in Colossians 1:22, in 1 Peter 1:19 (Christ as "a lamb without blemish and without spot"), and in Revelation 14:5. The LXX vocabulary of Leviticus 22 runs directly into the New Testament's language for what Christ is and what the redeemed community is called to become. The unblemished sacrifice becomes a lens through which both Christ's self-offering and the Christian vocation are understood.

The mother-offspring law of verse 28 is rendered with the Greek *me sphaxeis auto kai ta paideia autou en hemera mia* — "you shall not slaughter it and its young on the same day." The word *paideia* ordinarily means "children, young, offspring" and is also the word for "education, discipline, training" — derived from *pais* (child). The overlap is unintentional but suggestive: the compassion law that protects animal young is expressed in vocabulary that in other contexts speaks of the formation of children. Care for the young — in whatever form — belongs to the same moral imagination.

The climactic verse 32's *ve-niqdashthi* — "I will be made holy" — becomes in the LXX *kai hagiasthesomai* — "and I shall be sanctified." The passive voice is striking: God, in some grammatical sense, is sanctified by Israel's behavior. This is not a statement about God's essential holiness (which is underived and unconditional) but about the visibility and honor of that holiness in the world. God's holiness is *demonstrated*, *manifested*, *perceived as holy* through Israel's faithfulness. When Israel profanes what is sacred, God's holiness is hidden from the world. When Israel keeps the Holiness Code, God's holiness becomes visible. The stakes of the chapter are not Israel's welfare alone — they are the disclosure of the divine character in history.

The final *ani YHWH* of verse 33 is rendered *ego kyrios* — "I am the Lord." The LXX uses *kyrios* (Lord) throughout for YHWH, the divine name. In the New Testament, *kyrios* becomes the primary title for Jesus — a linguistic move that transfers the covenant formula of Leviticus ("I am the LORD") into the proclamation of the early church ("Jesus is Lord"). Every time Paul or Peter or the Gospel writers say *kyrios*, the echo of *ani YHWH* reverberates beneath the surface of the text.

---
*PNEUMA — translated from Leningrad Codex (B19A) folios 71r–71v, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) pages 108–109 (LXX).*
